author: @AmixLiark
text: The dual cone thing is genius. I would do 3 or 4 of them though to balance forces on the thrust rod. In the current setup, the torque converter will get squeezed above or below the two cones as you increase pressure to reduce slippage. Capturing it between 3-4 cones instead of 2 will prevent slippage, increase contact surface area and balance radial and sheer forces on the trust rod. If you do 4, you can have 2 input and 2 output and quite a lot less slippage.

author: @robmolenaar340
text: Hi, DAF used to make a car with such Constant Variable Transmission in the 60-70s in Holland. Used by "able" and "disabled" people alike. Added bonus: you could push-start it backwards and drive just as fast backwards as forwards! It made for fantastic "backwards racing days" on the F1circuit in Zandvoort!! Made great TV entertainment.

author: @Dr._Spamy
text: But this parallel blue "transmitting roller" doesn't run true, as it shows in the upper picture of this roller bearing at 2:50. It's just running true at the middle line of it's width. This blue transmitter roller also need to be a cone, to run true, like the lower one in the bearing picture.

author: @Kineth1
text: Growing up in the ninety's, we lived in Maine, and my parents had a snowblower that I would run to clear the driveway.  It had a drive system that took power from the engine to the disc component, and the roller drove a single, solid driveshaft for the two wheels.  Each wheel had an independent clutch that was engaged by gripping a lever on each handle.  Let go of the machine and it stops driving.  Squeeze the right lever and the right wheel drives, left lever and the left wheel drives.  I was really impressed by the mechanism, which was exposed during operation back then.  Even now, I think my favorite feature of that transmission style is its ability to go full speed forward and reverse, without the use of any additional components to reverse the output.

author: @VeniceInventors
text: For lack of a "best" solution, the "better" option seems to be combining different solutions so that the benefits of one approach cancel the compromises of the others. For example, using a ball bearing and two thrust bearings to handle both axial and radial loads. Other than the added friction, the remaining compromises (increased complexity and cost) don't affect the performance of the system itself. Or mixing lead and tin to make solder. Nature is full of inspiring examples where very different structures are combined to go beyond the limitations of each part, like bones and muscles.

author: @normang3668
text:
0:59 Funny to actually see an example of this transmission... I had this same idea (mind you I'm not terribly surprised to find out it already exists, as it's a pretty simple idea.) I was imagining it as a bicycle transmission, where the back wheel is the larger disk and a drive shaft moves across its radius to drive it...

Of course it still runs into the same problem every CVT has: It simultaneously needs grip to transfer the power, and slippage to be able to change speeds freely.

author: @dav1dbone
text: You might try "random z seam" printing cones, tubes etc, stops that annoying build line - saves/makes easier smoothing with files etc

author: @jdog0006
text: This transmission is absolutely brilliant. Since it is variable, it is better than a fixed ratio in almost all situations. For true variability, you don't necessarily need a second threaded shaft. You could simply thread the idler shaft to move the idler up and down for different ratios. I am fairly certain that I would prefer this to a fixed gear system for almost everything. Kudos for this.
